data.title: DocuSign signals Q4 revenue guidance of up to $829M while advancing IAM adoption and ARR reporting transition
data.description:
Earnings Call Insights: DocuSign (DOCU) Q3 2026

Management View

  • Allan Thygesen, President, CEO & Director, stated that "Q3 was a standout quarter for DocuSign. We delivered one of the higher growth quarters over the past 2 years, driven by continued customer investment in core products and the Intelligent Agreement Management, or IAM, platform." Thygesen highlighted revenue of $818 million, up 8% year-over-year, billings of $829 million, and a non-GAAP operating margin of 31%. Free cash flow reached $263 million, supporting a $215 million share repurchase, DocuSign's largest quarterly buyback.

  • Thygesen emphasized ongoing execution across three strategic pillars: meeting demand for IAM and eSignature with an improving go-to-market motion, maintaining rapid platform evolution and AI innovation, and driving greater operational efficiency. Over 25,000 customers had adopted IAM by Q3, increasing from over 10,000 in April, and IAM is expected to represent a low double-digit percentage of recurring revenue at year-end.

  • Thygesen noted, "IAM is a system of record that enables customers of all sizes to ingest a vast, complex body of agreements into a single repository, build agreement workflows that operate at scale and take action on high-accuracy insights from agreement data." He cited strong retention rates in IAM renewal cohorts and increasing eSignature usage among IAM customers. The company also reported international revenue at approximately 30% of business for the first time.

  • Product innovation was highlighted, with the launch of Agreement Desk and AI contract agents in beta. IAM is now integrated with platforms such as ChatGPT, Anthropic Claude, Gemini Enterprise, and others. IAM achieved FedRAMP Moderate and GovRAMP authorization in Q3.

  • Blake Grayson, Executive VP & CFO, stated, "Q3 results demonstrated another quarter of resiliency, with consistent overall growth in IAM demand momentum. We also continued to generate strong operating profitability and cash flow and translated that performance into our single largest quarterly dollar buyback in the company's history."

Outlook

  • For Q4 2026, DocuSign expects total revenue of $825 million to $829 million, a 7% year-over-year increase at the midpoint, and $3.208 billion to $3.212 billion for fiscal 2026, an 8% year-over-year increase at the midpoint. Subscription revenue is projected at $808 million to $812 million for Q4, and $3.140 billion to $3.144 billion for the year.

  • Billings guidance for Q4 is $992 million to $1.002 billion, or 8% growth at the midpoint, with full-year billings of $3.379 billion to $3.389 billion, a 9% increase at the midpoint. Non-GAAP gross margin is expected between 80.8% to 81.2% for Q4, and non-GAAP operating margin is projected at 28.3% to 28.7% for Q4. The company also announced it will transition from billings to ARR reporting in Q4 2026, providing annual ARR guidance and quarterly IAM contribution disclosures.

Financial Results

  • Total revenue in Q3 was $818 million and subscription revenue was $801 million, both up 8% and 9% year-over-year, respectively. Billings reached $829 million, up 10% year-over-year. Dollar net retention rate improved to 102%, consistent with Q2. Free cash flow was $263 million, with a 32% margin. The company ended Q3 with approximately $1 billion in cash, cash equivalents, and investments, and no debt. Non-GAAP diluted EPS for Q3 was $1.01, up from $0.90 last year, and GAAP diluted EPS was $0.40 versus $0.30 last year.

Q&A

  • Jacob Roberge, William Blair: Questioned the ARR transition and IAM renewal cohorts. Grayson responded that ARR will be disclosed in the March call and emphasized a positive trajectory driven by IAM and improved retention. Thygesen added, "We're running the business on ARR now...it's the right long-term metric for the company."

  • Tyler Radke, Citi: Asked about Navigator's use cases and monetization. Thygesen explained Navigator is foundational to IAM and is monetized as part of the platform, not separately, citing proprietary data advantages.

  • Mark Murphy, JPMorgan: Asked about envelope sent growth and utilization rates. Grayson said envelope sent growth has been consistent, with utilization rates at multiyear highs, indicating positive signs for future upsell opportunities.

  • Brent Thill, Jefferies: Pressed on double-digit growth. Thygesen indicated retention and expansion bookings—particularly through IAM—are the main levers to reach that goal.

Sentiment Analysis

  • Analysts focused on ARR transition, IAM adoption, and growth sustainability, showing a slightly positive but probing tone, especially regarding key metrics and expansion opportunities.

  • Management maintained a confident and constructive tone, using phrases such as "we are focused on pursuing the vast opportunity ahead" and "I think we feel really good both about the initial sale and about the adoption and the follow-on."

  • Compared to the previous quarter, both analysts and management remained confident, though analysts pressed more on monetization and the reporting transition.

Quarter-over-Quarter Comparison

  • This quarter saw an increase in revenue, billings, and free cash flow compared to Q2. Guidance for revenue and billings was raised, and the company previewed a reporting transition from billings to ARR. IAM's customer base grew markedly, and international revenue reached 30% of the total for the first time. Management maintained a constructive tone, with continued focus on product innovation and operational efficiency. Analysts’ questions shifted more toward monetization strategies and the implications of the ARR transition.

Risks and Concerns

  • Management identified renewal timing as a source of variability in billings and noted that timing fluctuations can impact ARR similarly. Grayson stated, "renewal timing can create significant variability in billings as a reporting metric."

  • The company flagged a challenging year-over-year comparison against prior periods due to early renewals and cloud migration headwinds impacting gross margin. Gradual easing of migration costs is expected in fiscal 2027 and beyond.

  • Analysts raised concerns about the sustainability of IAM growth, customer expansion dynamics, and the impact of reporting changes.

Final Takeaway

DocuSign’s Q3 performance reflected strong growth in IAM platform adoption, resilient core business metrics, and steady international expansion. Management raised both revenue and billings guidance while signaling a shift to ARR-based reporting to enhance transparency. The company emphasized continued product innovation and operational discipline as key drivers of sustainable, profitable growth, with the IAM platform positioned as a cornerstone for future expansion.

Read the full Earnings Call Transcript

data.title: Freeport-McMoRan not counting on M&A for growth, CEO says
data.description:
Freeport-McMoRan (FCX) would pursue an acquisition if "the stars and the moon" aligned, but is not counting on deals to grow and is focused on developing existing assets, CEO Kathleen Quick said Thursday at the Reuters NEXT conference in New York.

"You never say never about M&A, because we keep relationships around the industry... but we're not counting on that," Quirk said, according to Reuters. "The stars and the moon and everything would need to align."

Quick's view is something of an outlier among the biggest global miners, as global demand for copper has pushed rivals BHP, Anglo American and others to scramble for deals.

Freeport (FCX), which operates mines across North and South America and in Indonesia, would be interested only in buying a rival copper company or mine that could have synergies with its existing assets and technology, the CEO said.

Quick also said the U.S. government should do more to protect the domestic copper industry from competitive global threats; President Trump has imposed a 50% tariff on copper pipes, tubes, and other semi-finished products, but left out copper input materials such as ores, concentrates, and cathodes that Freeport (FCX) produces.

"If the U.S. wants to be self-sufficient in copper, some kind of incentive would help," the CEO said, adding that permitting reform is one of the company's other requests from the Trump administration.

data.title: HPE raises FY26 EPS guidance to $2.25–$2.45 with $1.7B–$2B free cash flow outlook as networking mix strengthens
data.description:
Earnings Call Insights: Hewlett Packard Enterprise (HPE) Q4 2025

Management View

  • Antonio Neri, CEO, described Q4 as a “record quarter of profitable growth and disciplined execution,” reporting Q4 revenue of $9.7 billion, up 14% year-over-year, and non-GAAP operating profit up 26%. Neri stated, “Stronger profitability also resulted in higher-than-expected free cash flow of $1.9 billion for the quarter, capping a solid fiscal year '25 performance.” He highlighted a significant acceleration in orders at quarter end, signaling robust underlying demand, and announced a raised fiscal year '26 non-GAAP diluted net EPS guidance as well as an increased midpoint for free cash flow.
  • Neri underscored strategic priorities: “building a new networking industry leader, profitably capturing the AI infrastructure build-out opportunity, accelerating our high-margin software and services growth through our GreenLake Cloud, capitalizing on the unstructured data market growth with our leading Alletra MP Storage offerings and driving the transition to our next-generation server platforms.”
  • The CEO confirmed the sale of HPE’s remaining 19% stake in H3C for approximately $1.4 billion, expected to close in the first half of 2026, supporting plans to reduce net leverage to around 2x by fiscal year-end 2027.
  • Neri emphasized the impact of the Juniper Networks acquisition, stating, “We completed the acquisition of Juniper Networks, strengthening our position in the networking market to create a new industry leader,” and that the integration progress is “significant,” with unified teams, technologies, and a positive response from employees, customers, and partners.
  • Marie Myers, CFO, stated, “We have established a robust foundation to transform our cost structure through catalyst initiatives, which combined with the synergies from Juniper, are targeting approximately $1 billion in annualized structural savings by fiscal 2028.”

Outlook

  • HPE reaffirmed its FY 2026 revenue growth outlook range of 17% to 22% on a reported basis or 5% to 10% on a pro forma basis, with revenue mix weighted more toward the second half of the year.
  • The company raised its full-year networking revenue growth outlook to 65% to 70% on a reported basis, implying approximately $11 billion in revenue.
  • Myers announced, “We are raising our fiscal 2026 non-GAAP diluted net EPS outlook range to $2.25 to $2.45.”
  • Free cash flow guidance for FY 2026 was increased to $1.7 billion to $2 billion, reflecting “faster-than-expected benefits from the integration of Juniper.”
  • Q1 2026 revenue is expected between $9 billion and $9.4 billion, with non-GAAP diluted net EPS between $0.57 and $0.61.

Financial Results

  • Revenue for Q4 was $9.7 billion, up 14% year-over-year and 6% sequentially. Non-GAAP diluted net EPS was $0.62, and free cash flow was $1.9 billion.
  • Networking segment delivered $2.8 billion in revenue, a 150% year-over-year increase, and a 62% sequential rise, with an operating margin of 23%. Myers noted, “Integration synergies have already been materializing, enabling Juniper to deliver an 8-year high in operating profit margin during Q4.”
  • Server revenue was $4.5 billion, a 5% decline year-over-year and 10% sequentially, mainly due to timing of AI server shipments and lower U.S. federal spending.
  • Hybrid Cloud revenue was $1.4 billion, down 13% year-over-year.
  • Annualized recurring revenue (ARR) grew to $3.2 billion, up 62% year-over-year with Juniper’s contribution.

Q&A

  • Amit Daryanani, Evercore: Asked about memory headwinds and the drivers of raised EPS and free cash flow guidance. CEO Neri responded, highlighting confidence in the new guidance, stating drivers include “the mix of the business, obviously, networking revenue increased now to the mid-single digits and the continuous actions that we take across the company... Catalyst is slightly ahead of what we wanted to be.” Myers added that revenue shifted from Q4 to Q1 contributed to the raised guidance.
  • Samik Chatterjee, JPMorgan: Inquired about the order acceleration and price actions. Neri explained strong order acceleration across the portfolio, noting, “it's early to say if there was a pull-in of sorts on demand,” but cited prudent pricing actions in response to DRAM cost signals.
  • Timothy Long, Barclays: Asked about ARR and GreenLake traction. Neri detailed that “all the ARR we added from Juniper is in the software subscription services,” and 80% of ARR is now software and services.
  • Erik Woodring, Morgan Stanley: Questioned DRAM cost pass-through and demand elasticity. Neri confirmed price increases to match DRAM costs and commented, “we are going to monitor the cost and the demand. And I think you will see a rebalancing over time between units and revenue.” Myers emphasized the use of demand shaping and pricing tools.
  • Wamsi Mohan, BofA: Sought clarity on revenue seasonality. Myers clarified that Q1 is consistent with historic seasonality despite some AI deals moving from Q4 to Q1. Neri elaborated on the pacing and timing of AI shipments.
  • Aaron Rakers, Wells Fargo: Probed networking growth deceleration. Myers cited prudent guidance given ongoing sales force integration and seasonality. Neri stated, “we believe there is a tremendous opportunity. And the goal there is...all about execution.”
  • Asiya Merchant, Citi: Asked about the H3C asset sale and OI&E. Myers confirmed expectations are captured in current OI&E guidance.

Sentiment Analysis

  • Analysts pressed on the sustainability of raised guidance, the impact of component costs, and demand elasticity, with a slightly skeptical tone regarding the durability of recent order acceleration and price pass-through.
  • Management maintained a confident and detailed tone during prepared remarks, with Neri stating, “we are very confident in the new guide we provided,” but shifted to more explanatory and cautious language during Q&A, frequently referencing prudent planning and real-time monitoring of trends.
  • Compared to the previous quarter, both analysts and management demonstrated increased focus on cost inflation and order timing, with management showing higher confidence in guidance but more caution regarding execution risks.

Quarter-over-Quarter Comparison

  • The current quarter saw HPE raise its FY 2026 EPS and free cash flow guidance, while maintaining overall revenue growth targets. In the previous quarter, guidance was more conservative, and networking growth expectations were lower.
  • Strategic focus has shifted more clearly to networking as the central pillar of the business, following the Juniper integration, with increased emphasis on software and recurring revenue.
  • Analysts in both quarters focused on post-acquisition integration, margin progression, and AI-related opportunity, but current quarter questions showed heightened concern about commodity cost impacts and the sustainability of demand trends.
  • Management’s tone this quarter is more confident regarding profit outlook but more attentive to risks like component costs and deal conversion timing.

Risks and Concerns

  • Management cited industry-wide DRAM and NAND cost increases, with actions underway to pass costs to customers and monitor demand elasticity.
  • Revenue and margin performance are sensitive to timing of AI server shipments and the lumpiness in sovereign and enterprise customer deal conversions.
  • Integration of Juniper remains a top priority, with execution risk acknowledged around the sales force merger and realization of synergies.
  • AI demand is expected to remain uneven, as some larger sovereign customers are placing orders with extended lead times, potentially deferring shipments.
  • Analyst concerns centered on the potential for demand pull-in, pricing power sustainability, and the impact of commodity inflation on margins.

Final Takeaway

Hewlett Packard Enterprise closed a transformative fiscal year by delivering robust growth in networking, improved profitability, and strong free cash flow, bolstered by the integration of Juniper Networks and disciplined cost controls. With networking now positioned as the company’s central growth engine, HPE raised its FY 2026 EPS and free cash flow outlooks, reflecting confidence in the company’s technology leadership and execution. Management remains focused on navigating commodity cost pressures, capturing AI infrastructure opportunities, and driving value through software and services expansion, while monitoring order trends and integration milestones as it enters the new fiscal year.

Read the full Earnings Call Transcript

data.title: Crude oil climbs as Ukraine war drags on, U.S. rate cut seen as imminent
data.description:
Crude oil futures rose Thursday as the lack of progress in Russia-Ukraine peace talks points to the war in Ukraine dragging on, reducing the likelihood that large volumes of Russian crude oil will return to the global market anytime soon, while markets expect the U.S. Federal Reserve to cut interest rates next week, supporting economic growth and oil demand.

"Ukraine's drone campaign against Russian refining infrastructure has shifted into a more sustained and strategically coordinated phase," Kpler said in a research report. "This has pushed Russian refining throughput down to around 5 million bbl/day between September and November, a 335,000 bbl/day Y/Y decline, with gasoline hit hardest and gasoil output also materially weaker."

An imminent quarter-point rate cut by the Fed, considered all but certain, has strengthened the outlook for higher energy demand in 2025, while the weakness of the U.S. dollar - down for the 10th straight day Thursday against a basket of major currencies - makes crude imports cheaper for international buyers using other currencies.

Analysts said rising tensions between the U.S. and Venezuela also supported prices on concerns of a drop in crude supplies from the South American country.

Still, concerns over excess supply continue to hang over global markets, and Saudi Arabia set the January Arab Light crude oil official selling price to Asia at $0.60/bbl above the Oman/Dubai average, down from $1 bbl last month; the price is seen as a key indicator of the Saudi outlook on regional demand.

Front-month Nymex crude (CL1:COM) for January closed +1.2% to $59.67/bbl, and front-month Brent crude (CO1:COM) for February delivery ended +0.9% to $63.26/bbl, the highest settlement value since November 18 and November 27, respectively.

U.S. natural gas futures (NG1:COM) closed above $5 for the first time since 2022, as cold temperatures boost demand for heating, with the Nymex front-month January contract finishing +1.3% to $5.063/MMBtu, its best settlement value since December 27, 2022.

ETFs: (USO), (BNO), (UCO), (SCO), (USL), (DBO), (DRIP), (GUSH), (USOI), (UNG), (BOIL), (KOLD), (UNL), (FCG), (XLE)

data.title: IDT outlines $141M–$145M adjusted EBITDA target for fiscal 2026 while advancing AI and premium services
data.description:
Earnings Call Insights: IDT Corporation (IDT) Q1 2026

Management View

  • CEO Samuel Jonas stated that "IDT delivered consolidated revenue growth and record levels of gross profit, adjusted EBITDA -- and adjusted EBITDA in the first quarter. NRS led top line expansion, while all 3 of our growth segments reported strong bottom line results. Our Traditional Communications segment again provided steady cash generation." Jonas highlighted that "NRS recurring revenue increased 22% year-over-year, helping to drive a 35% increase in income from operations and a 33% increase in adjusted EBITDA." He also discussed the rollout of "innovative premium services, delivery integrations, couponing and product data scan programs" and emphasized the growing importance of premium offerings in driving average recurring revenue per terminal.
  • Jonas noted, "Our push to integrate tailored AI and machine learning into BOSS Money customer service and fraud detection activities have helped to significantly improve unit economics." The company is introducing BOSS Wallet, soon enabling U.S. customers to share money and receive rewards. He also stated, "During the quarter, net2phone began offering its AI agent to both our existing and new customers and added our Coach AI solution at quarter's end," marking a pivot toward holistic solutions over stand-alone products for net2phone.
  • Jonas acknowledged the favorable resolution of the Straight Path class action, stating, "the Delaware Supreme Court in a ruling issued yesterday affirmed the decision of the Court of Chancery dismissing all claims against IDT in the Straight Path class action suit, and we are very pleased that this case has now been favorably resolved."
  • CFO Marcelo Fischer stated, "From a financial perspective, this was a terrific quarter, highlighted by good top line growth, record gross profit and record adjusted EBITDA and adjusted EBITDA margin." Fischer reported, "Consolidated revenue increased 4% to $323 million... Consolidated gross profit increased 10% to a record $118 million for a gross margin of 37%... Consolidated income from operations increased to $31 million, a 31% year-over-year increase. Adjusted EBITDA and adjusted EBITDA margin also hit record levels at $37.9 million and 11.7%, respectively."

Outlook

  • Fischer concluded, "after generating $38 million in consolidated adjusted EBITDA this quarter, representing a 26% year-over-year growth, IDT is extremely well positioned to achieve our full year '26 adjusted EBITDA guidance of $141 million to $145 million, which would represent a 7% to 10% full year-over-year growth rate." The company will monitor Q2 performance and update guidance in early March.
  • Jonas explained that NRS and BOSS Money remain positioned for long-term growth through continued innovation and market expansion, with net2phone expected to increase technology investments for new verticals such as health care.

Financial Results

  • Fischer reported that "Consolidated revenue increased 4% to $323 million," with the three growth segments—NRS, Fintech, and net2phone—growing by 16%. He highlighted that "EPS increased by 31% or $0.21 to $0.89 per share on both the basic and diluted basis. Non-GAAP diluted EPS also climbed by 32% to $0.94 from $0.71."
  • NRS recurring revenue climbed 22% to $35 million, with merchant services revenue up 38% and SaaS fees up 30%, offsetting a 15% decline in advertising and data revenue. Income from operations in NRS increased 35% to $9 million, while adjusted EBITDA increased 33% to $10.3 million.
  • BOSS Money digital channel generated 84% of transactions, with revenue growth at 20%. Fintech segment income from operations increased 97% to $6 million, and adjusted EBITDA climbed 87% to $7.5 million.
  • Net2phone seat growth reached 432,000, revenue increased 10%, income from operations increased 94% to $2 million, and adjusted EBITDA increased 44% to $3.6 million.
  • Traditional Communications segment income from operations increased 1% year-over-year to $16 million; adjusted EBITDA increased 2% to $18.9 million.
  • Cash, cash equivalents, debt securities, and current equity investments totaled $220 million at quarter end, reflecting a $34 million decrease from the previous quarter due to timing of BOSS Money remittance funding. The company repurchased $7.6 million in stock during the quarter.

Q&A

  • Iñigo Alonso, MORAM Capital: Questioned capital allocation strategy in light of the Straight Path litigation resolution. Jonas answered, "we're not looking at anything very large right now unless something regulatorily changes... On the NRS front, we're not looking at any major acquisitions, but we do have 1 or 2 small acquisitions in mind for them."
  • Alonso asked about net payment processing account additions. Jonas replied, "It's coming from ones that require a POS."
  • Alonso inquired about adjacent growth markets for NRS. Jonas stated, "there's a bunch of adjacent markets... related to food service and related to international markets that we've yet to really launch in with the exception of Canada... if we build out a couple of small features for each one of them, they open up tens of thousands of stores each."
  • Alonso queried international expansion. Jonas responded, "I can't say 100% that we have decided to go into more countries yet. We're looking at an acquisition outside the country that would accelerate that for us. But it's definitely on the road map."
  • Alonso asked about record IDT Global top line. Jonas explained, "they're doing a great job all around bringing lots of new and interesting solution to our carrier partners all around the world... despite that the minutes business have been in decline now for so many years, they have consistently delivered what we look from a managerial perspective, about $9 million to $10 million of GP or gross profit every quarter for now several years."

Sentiment Analysis

  • Analysts expressed positive sentiment, highlighting the company's strong EBITDA growth and resolution of litigation. Questions focused on capital allocation, new markets, and growth strategies, with a tone of constructive curiosity.
  • Management maintained a confident tone in prepared remarks and Q&A, using phrases like "we are very excited about the potential" and "extremely well positioned to achieve our full year '26 adjusted EBITDA guidance." No defensive or hesitant language was observed.
  • Compared to the previous quarter, both analysts and management sustained a positive tone, with management maintaining strong confidence in growth segments and analysts focusing on opportunities for expansion and capital deployment.

Quarter-over-Quarter Comparison

  • Guidance for adjusted EBITDA remained at $141 million to $145 million for fiscal 2026, consistent with previous quarter statements.
  • Strategic focus continues to shift toward premium services, AI integration, and holistic solution offerings, notably in NRS, BOSS Money, and net2phone.
  • Key metrics such as recurring revenue growth, adjusted EBITDA, and operating leverage from high-margin growth segments all showed year-over-year increases.
  • Management's confidence level remained strong, particularly regarding technology investment and new product introductions, while analysts continued to probe on capital allocation and market expansion opportunities.

Risks and Concerns

  • Jonas noted the upcoming implementation of a new federal excise tax on cash remittances, which could "accelerate" churn in BOSS Money's retail channel.
  • Management is monitoring potential impacts from regulatory changes and is cautious on large-scale M&A, opting instead to focus on organic growth and small acquisitions.
  • The company referenced a decrease in cash reserves due to operational timing but remains committed to returning capital via repurchases and dividends.

Final Takeaway

IDT Corporation emphasized a strong start to fiscal 2026 with record quarterly performance across revenue, gross profit, and adjusted EBITDA, driven by strategic investments in premium services, AI integration, and a focus on high-margin growth segments. Management reiterated its confidence in meeting full year adjusted EBITDA guidance of $141 million to $145 million and outlined continued innovation in NRS, Fintech, and net2phone, while maintaining a disciplined approach to capital allocation and monitoring evolving regulatory factors in remittance markets.

Read the full Earnings Call Transcript

data.title: SentinelOne signals durable growth with $1B FY 2026 revenue outlook as CFO transition begins
data.description:
Earnings Call Insights: SentinelOne (S) Q3 2026

Management View

  • Tomer Weingarten, CEO, reported "Q3 was another strong quarter for SentinelOne. We exceeded our top and bottom line expectations, demonstrating durable growth and continued operating leverage." ARR grew 23% year-over-year, with net new ARR also growing both year-over-year and sequentially. The company highlighted expanding market share, strong adoption of the AI-driven Singularity platform, and progress toward sustainable profitability.
  • Weingarten detailed accelerating adoption of emerging platform solutions, noting "our non-Endpoint solutions represented approximately half of quarterly bookings," and record ARR per customer, largely driven by data, Purple AI, and Cloud Security Solutions.
  • Purple AI achieved a "record attach rate that surpassed 40% of licenses sold in Q3" and was described as a cornerstone for modern security operations. Bookings growth for data solutions accelerated to triple digits year-over-year. Cloud Security had its strongest bookings growth in the last 12 months.
  • The company completed the acquisition of Observo AI for $225 million, which Weingarten described as delivering "an AI-native real-time telemetry pipeline" and enabling control over the data pipeline for security operations.
  • SentinelOne announced the departure of CFO Barbara Larson, who will remain through mid-January for transition. Barry Padgett, Chief Growth Officer, will serve as Interim CFO. Weingarten said, "Barbara will remain with us through mid-January to ensure a seamless transition."
  • CFO Barbara Larson stated, "In Q3, we outperformed our guidance on both top and bottom line metrics. Our total ARR grew 23%, and we added net new ARR of $54 million in the quarter, which exceeded our expectations." She added, "We achieved operating profitability of 7% in the quarter... and our highest quarterly net income margin, which increased to 10% in Q3, significantly higher from breakeven in the prior year quarter."

Outlook

  • SentinelOne expects fiscal year 2026 revenue to be approximately $1.001 billion, representing 22% year-over-year growth and raising the midpoint of prior guidance by $1 million. Q4 revenue is expected to be about $271 million, or 20% year-over-year growth.
  • Larson guided, "For the full year, we expect gross margin to be approximately 78.5%. And for Q4, we expect gross margin to be approximately 77.5%." Operating margin for the full year is expected to slightly exceed 3%, with Q4 operating margin at approximately 5%. Strategic acquisitions and FX headwinds will impact operating margin by a combined 250 basis points for the year.
  • The company reaffirmed its commitment to delivering positive free cash flow for the full year.

Financial Results

  • Q3 revenue grew 23% year-over-year to $259 million. International markets grew 34% and represented 40% of total revenue. Customers with ARR of $100,000 or more increased 20% to 1,572.
  • Gross margin remained at 79%. Operating margin reached 7%. Free cash flow margin was 6% for the quarter. Remaining Performance Obligations (RPO) grew 35% to $1.3 billion.
  • The acquisition of Observo AI is expected to have a minimal top-line impact in fiscal 2026 and a 60 basis point impact to full year operating margin.

Q&A

  • Saket Kalia, Barclays: Asked about non-endpoint product traction and Flex's role. CEO Weingarten responded, "Data Solutions... accelerated for us to triple-digit year-over-year growth... Purple AI... about 40% attached, which is a new record for us... Flex enables that consumption across the board."
  • John DiFucci, Guggenheim: Asked about lighter guidance and the CFO transition. Larson replied, "Q4 revenue outlook reflects steady momentum... things like deal timing, in-quarter linearity can influence quarterly revenue... this is just really a personal decision for me."
  • Brian Essex, JPMorgan: Asked about gross margin guidance. Larson said, "Q3, we delivered industry-leading gross margins of 79%. We expect to sustain gross margin at the high 70s level... Q4 guide includes strategic investments in cloud infrastructure and capacity expansion."
  • Nasr Islam, Deutsche Bank: Asked about potential for further M&A. Weingarten stated, "We believe we have a pretty complete platform... We're always going to remain opportunistic. But... we don't envision any big missing pieces."
  • Meta Marshall, Morgan Stanley: Asked about execution and Flex. Weingarten explained, "Definitely steady execution, a lot of platform momentum... Flex is already helping us secure larger multi-solution deals and longer-term customer commitments."
  • Other questions covered ARR growth expectations, net retention, deal linearity, AI security adoption, actions to accelerate ARR, partner ecosystem, and competitive displacement.

Sentiment Analysis

  • Analysts' tone was slightly positive, expressing congratulations on strong performance but raising questions about the lighter Q4 revenue guide, gross margin compression, and the impact of the CFO transition.
  • Management maintained confidence in prepared remarks and during Q&A, frequently using phrases such as "we are confident" and "we believe," and offered detailed explanations for guidance and operational decisions. During Q&A, management was measured and reiterated commitment to growth and profitability, with no significant defensiveness detected.
  • Compared to the previous quarter, analyst skepticism over guidance and the CFO change was more pronounced, while management’s tone remained consistently confident and focused on execution.

Quarter-over-Quarter Comparison

  • Revenue growth rate remained stable at 22-23% year-over-year. Operating margin improved from 2% in Q2 to 7% in Q3. Free cash flow margin also improved quarter-over-quarter.
  • Guidance for full-year revenue increased slightly. Gross margin guidance moderated marginally due to investments in cloud infrastructure.
  • Strategic focus shifted further toward non-endpoint platform adoption, emerging product adoption, and integrating acquisitions.
  • Analysts in Q3 were more focused on guidance conservatism, CFO transition, and the impact of strategic investments on margins, compared to Q2's focus on ARR drivers and Flex adoption.
  • Management’s confidence in platform momentum and profitability was consistent, though the CFO transition introduced new questions.

Risks and Concerns

  • Management cited macroeconomic uncertainty impacting deal timing and sales cycles, particularly in seasonally significant Q4.
  • Gross margin is expected to compress slightly due to investments in cloud infrastructure and global expansion.
  • The CFO transition was addressed, with management emphasizing continuity and a strong finance team in place.
  • Minimal revenue and ARR contribution expected from recent acquisitions in fiscal 2026, with short-term margin impact.

Final Takeaway

SentinelOne delivered another quarter of robust growth, expanding its platform reach and demonstrating operational leverage, while also signaling confidence in its trajectory toward durable profitability. The company raised its full-year revenue outlook to $1.001 billion, highlighted record adoption of non-endpoint solutions including Purple AI and Data Solutions, and completed the acquisition of Observo AI to strengthen its data pipeline capabilities. Despite the announced CFO transition, leadership underscored continuity and reaffirmed the commitment to profitable growth and innovation in AI-driven cybersecurity.

Read the full Earnings Call Transcript

data.title: New Fortress Energy wins Puerto Rico approval for long-term gas supply
data.description:
New Fortress Energy (NFE) +17.1% post-market Thursday after saying it received final approval for a seven-year gas agreement from Puerto Rico's Financial Oversight and Management Board to supply liquefied natural gas to the island.

The contract, valued at an estimated $3.2 billion, will secure the delivery of ~75 TBtu of natural gas to help enhance grid stability and support cleaner power generation across Puerto Rico's energy system, New Fortress (NFE) said.

Earlier this week, the contract received conditional approval from the Puerto Rico regulator after an intense review.

Two weeks ago, the company warned it may seek U.S. bankruptcy protection if it fails in its efforts to negotiate an out-of-court restructuring with lenders.

data.title: ChargePoint outlines $100M–$110M Q4 revenue target as debt reduction and new products drive growth
data.description:
Earnings Call Insights: ChargePoint Holdings, Inc. (CHPT) Q3 2026

Management View

  • Richard Wilmer, President, CEO & Director, stated that "Financial performance this quarter exceeded expectations. Revenue surpassed the top end of our guidance, reaching $106 million, which marks a return to growth." He emphasized ongoing momentum with new products, citing the Eaton partnership and European expansion as key growth drivers for the second half of calendar 2026. Wilmer highlighted, "Our non-GAAP gross margin remained at a record high of 33%. We maintained strict cash discipline with cash utilization better than planned at $14 million." He also noted a completed debt exchange, securing nearly $110 million of deal discounts, reducing outstanding debt by $172 million and extending maturity to 2030. Wilmer described this as "a pivotal step in strengthening our financial foundation."
  • Wilmer detailed product and market developments, including the extension of a partnership with the City of New York and a new program with BMW North America for destination charging stations. He asserted, "ChargePoint now manages approximately 375,000 ports including more than 39,000 DC fast chargers and more than 127,000 ports located in Europe."
  • Wilmer commented on innovation: "Our new DC product line ChargePoint Express powered by Eaton is a bidirectional capable solution that we believe can be deployed with up to 30% lower capital expenditure, occupies a 30% smaller footprint and reduces ongoing operational costs by up to 30% compared to other solutions."
  • Mansi Khetani, CFO & Chief Accounting Officer, stated, "Revenue for the third quarter exceeded our expectations coming in at $106 million, significantly above the high end of our guidance range of $90 million to $100 million up 7% sequentially and up 6% year-on-year." She highlighted that "Non-GAAP gross margin remained at a record high of 33%, flat sequentially and up 7 percentage points year-on-year." Khetani added, "Non-GAAP operating expenses were $57 million, representing a 2% reduction, both sequentially and year-on-year."

Outlook

  • Khetani provided guidance: "For the fourth quarter of fiscal 2026, we expect revenue to be $100 million to $110 million representing a 3% year-on-year growth at the midpoint." She stated, "While we remain cautious in light of the broader macroeconomic environment, we are confident that revenue growth will continue as we execute on our strategic priorities."
  • Management indicated ongoing focus on new product ramp, European expansion, and the Eaton partnership as growth catalysts for the second half of 2026.

Financial Results

  • Khetani reported, "Subscription revenue at $42 million was 40% of total revenue up 5% sequentially and up 15% year-on-year as our total installed base continues to grow. Other revenue at $7 million was 7% of total revenue."
  • Non-GAAP adjusted EBITDA loss was $19 million, improving from $22 million in the prior quarter and $29 million in the third quarter of last year. Stock-based compensation was $15 million, down from $18 million last quarter.
  • Inventory balance was stable at $212 million. Cash balance was $181 million compared to $195 million in the prior quarter, reflecting cash usage of $14 million.
  • Khetani described the debt exchange: "We completed a privately negotiated debt exchange with existing holders that will ultimately reduce our total debt by $172 million, more than half of the previous balance... and extension of debt maturity from 2028 to 2030."

Q&A

  • Colin Rusch, Oppenheimer: Asked about product evolution and demand from virtual power plants. Wilmer responded, "The new Flex product line... will start rolling out in 2026... our new Express line... can integrate directly with the DC grid... provides not only full bidirectional charging but very significant economic benefits in terms of CapEx and OpEx."
  • Rusch: Follow-up on inventory reduction. Khetani answered, "We will see a small decline in Q4, most likely in the inventory balance but we expect a more material decrease throughout next fiscal year as we sell through the existing inventory."
  • Mark Delaney, Goldman Sachs: Inquired on gross margin potential with new products. Khetani said, "Improvements in hardware margin in the near term will be entirely driven by product mix... we expect a larger improvement from Asia manufacturing as we sell through our existing inventory... that should come in towards the latter half of next year."
  • Christopher Pierce, Needham: Asked about confidence in European projects and timing. Wilmer explained, "I was in Europe recently... the response was overwhelmingly positive... I'm quite confident that we'll win a number of fairly significant deals in Europe as we bring that product to market in the second half of next year."
  • William Peterson, JPMorgan: Queried about revenue beat and profitability. Khetani noted, "The significant beat was mostly due to a boost in residential billings due to the expiration of the federal EV credits... we're not guiding to a time frame, but EBITDA... will come with growth in revenue which we are significantly focused on."
  • Craig Irwin, ROTH Capital: Asked about NEVI funding and state support. Wilmer replied, "With respect to NEVI, we are seeing projects move forward... 40 states now are active in NEVI and awarding contracts, and we're active in many of those."

Sentiment Analysis

  • Analysts raised questions focused on inventory management, margin improvements, European growth, and the sustainability of recent revenue gains, reflecting a cautiously optimistic but probing tone.
  • Management responded with a confident and constructive tone, emphasizing product innovation, partnerships, margin resiliency, and prudent expense control. Phrases such as "we are confident that revenue growth will continue" and "our solutions do more than meet expectations they are redefining them" underscored this.
  • Compared to the previous quarter, both analysts and management exhibited a more positive tone, with management showing increased confidence and analysts concentrating on tangible growth catalysts and risk mitigation.

Quarter-over-Quarter Comparison

  • Revenue grew from $99 million in Q2 to $106 million in Q3, with non-GAAP gross margin steady at 33%.
  • Guidance language shifted from cautious optimism to a firmer expectation of growth, supported by debt reduction and new product launches.
  • Analysts' focus moved from concerns about macroeconomic headwinds and delays to concrete questions about inventory reduction, margin gains, and European expansion.
  • Management’s confidence increased, reflected in detailed commentary on innovation, financial discipline, and partnership execution. Analyst sentiment became less skeptical, focusing more on operational execution and upside from new initiatives.

Risks and Concerns

  • Management acknowledged ongoing macroeconomic uncertainty and the importance of prudent expense management.
  • Khetani addressed inventory risk, noting strategic decisions to wind down certain manufacturer commitments and expectation of gradual inventory reduction.
  • Wilmer mentioned industry consolidation as a trend, which could present both risks and opportunities for ChargePoint’s market position.

Final Takeaway

ChargePoint's third quarter marked a return to growth, driven by new product launches, an expanded partnership with Eaton, and accelerating opportunities in Europe. The company reported record gross margins, disciplined cash management, and a transformative debt reduction that strengthens its balance sheet and positions it for future growth. Management is confident in its ability to deliver innovation, capture market share, and improve margins, with clear expectations for continued revenue gains into the next quarter and beyond.

Read the full Earnings Call Transcript

data.title: Smith & Wesson projects Q3 sales growth of 8%–10% amid inventory reduction and robust new product momentum
data.description:
Earnings Call Insights: Smith & Wesson Brands, Inc. (SWBI) Q2 2026

Management View

  • Mark Smith, President, CEO & Director, stated that "excellent efficiency in our business operations allowed us to deliver solid profitability of $15 million of EBITDAS on net sales of nearly $125 million." He highlighted a "significant reduction in inventory," generating "healthy operating cash flow of over $27 million in the quarter." New products accounted for "nearly 40% of sales in the quarter." Smith emphasized that "handgun outperformance far outweighed the impact of long gun seasonality," with overall average selling prices (ASPs) rising 3.5% year-over-year and sequentially up 6.5%.
  • Smith reported that "channel inventory at distributors continues to be very clean declining over 5% sequentially and over 15% year-on-year, positioning us to quickly convert incremental demand into shipments as we move into our typically busy second half of the fiscal year."
  • Smith provided an update on the new Smith & Wesson Academy, stating, "just over 2 months in, we have already had the pleasure of hosting dozens of current and prospective law enforcement customers and held our first consumer training classes. The feedback has been overwhelmingly positive."
  • Deana McPherson, Executive VP, CFO, Treasurer & Assistant Secretary, noted, "Net sales for our second quarter of $124.7 million were $5 million or 3.9% below the prior year." She reported that "gross margin of 24.3% was down 2.3% versus a year ago, due primarily to decreased absorption on temporarily lower production as we focus on inventory optimization and an 80 basis point negative impact from tariffs." McPherson added, "the lower revenue and associated margin resulted in net income of $1.9 million compared with $4.5 million in the prior year period. Earnings per share during the second quarter was $0.04 compared with $0.10 a year ago."

Outlook

  • McPherson stated, "we expect our third quarter sales will be 8% to 10% over our Q3 fiscal 2025 sales with no significant impact either positively or negatively from channel inventory."
  • She explained that "Q3 gross margins to increase by a few percentage points, both sequentially and year-over-year. Operating expenses in Q3 will likely be about 15% higher than in Q2 with increases due to the SHOT Show in January, new product development costs, increased promotions and increased profit sharing." The company also expects "continued healthy cash generation through the second half of the fiscal year. Our effective tax rate is expected to be approximately 28%."

Financial Results

  • McPherson reported that "hand gun ASPs increased slightly from Q1 levels due to strong demand for certain premium products, partially offset by promotions and continued demand for lower-priced products. Long gun ASPs increased due to the mix of higher-priced products and slightly increased overall volume."
  • Operating expenses were $26.2 million, which was $733,000 lower than a year ago, with the increase in selling and marketing costs for the Academy grand opening offset by lower G&A primarily due to lower legal costs.
  • Cash generated from operations was $27.3 million compared with cash used from operations of $7.4 million in the prior year quarter due primarily to lower inventory and income taxes paid. Inventory decreased $20 million versus an increase of $6.2 million in the prior year quarter.
  • The company spent $11 million on capital projects, paid $5.8 million in dividends, ended the quarter with $27.3 million in cash and investments, and had $90 million in borrowings on its line of credit. Since quarter end, $15 million has been repaid, bringing borrowings to $75 million.

Q&A

  • Alex Sturnieks, Lake Street Capital Markets: "You noted an 80-point or 80 basis point headwind in the quarter. But could you just walk us through what you're seeing in input costs right now, steel components, tariffs and how you're thinking about gross margins over the next couple of quarters?" Mark Smith answered that "our impact from tariffs, you can probably expect it to pick up a little bit as we go through the back half of the year just as we work through some of the inventory that we had already in stock from kind of the pre-tariff days. But it shouldn't have a material impact on our profitability as we go through the back half." Deana McPherson added, "given that inventory has declined and we're now ramping back up, the absorption will probably be a little bit favorable. So you'll see a little bit of a positive impact that should be able to offset that impact of tariff costs."
  • Sturnieks: "OpEx looked really clean this quarter, specifically G&A. Is this a level you feel you can hold on to? Or should we expect G&A to tick up as we move through the rest of the year?" Mark Smith replied, "our operating expenses are usually fairly consistent year-to-year. So we always have an increase for SHOT Show in January... We're pretty disciplined in managing the OpEx line in general."
  • Sturnieks: "It sounds like you're seeing some nice tailwinds given the Q3 outlook. Any early thoughts on how Q4 is shaping up from where you sit today?" Mark Smith responded, "Q4 always is our strongest quarter. And this year, I don't think it's going to be any different. I think you can expect somewhere high single-digit, low double-digit growth in Q4 over Q3 this year."
  • Rommel Dionisio, Aegis Capital: "I wonder if you could just give us a little heads up in terms of the feedback you're receiving with regards to reset for new products, outlook for calendar 2026 in the industry overall?" Mark Smith answered, "the conversations we've been on with our -- whether it's distributors, retailers or all of our channel partners have been very positive around Smith & Wesson... Their inventory is very clean across the line and performing efficiently for them."

Sentiment Analysis

  • Analysts focused on cost headwinds, operating expense discipline, and market outlook, with a generally neutral but inquisitive tone, seeking clarification on input costs and margin visibility.
  • Management maintained a confident tone during both prepared remarks and Q&A, emphasizing efficiency, inventory discipline, and product momentum. Phrases such as "we believe we are well positioned for continued success" and "the strength of the brand is really starting to show through" reflected ongoing confidence.
  • Compared to the previous quarter, both analysts and management sentiment remained steady, with management slightly more upbeat on inventory progress and product acceptance.

Quarter-over-Quarter Comparison

  • Guidance for Q3 shifted from a general expectation of "normal seasonal environment" and sales growth over Q1 (in Q1 call) to a specific target of "8% to 10% over our Q3 fiscal 2025 sales."
  • Management's tone was more confident regarding inventory management, noting progress in reducing inventory and positioning for the seasonally strong second half.
  • Analyst focus shifted from product pricing strategies and legacy product inventory (Q1) to input cost pressures, margin outlook, and operational expense discipline (Q2).
  • Key metrics such as net sales, gross margin, and EPS were lower than the prior year quarter but marked a sequential improvement from Q1.
  • Strategic priorities remained constant, with a continued emphasis on new product innovation, channel inventory management, and capital discipline.

Risks and Concerns

  • Management highlighted the ongoing risk of tariffs impacting gross margins, though they stated, "it shouldn't have a material impact on our profitability as we go through the back half."
  • Macroeconomic uncertainty was noted as a factor that could affect performance in the coming quarters.
  • Analysts probed for possible increases in input costs and the sustainability of current operating expense levels.
  • Management's mitigation strategies include disciplined cost management, leveraging operational efficiency, and maintaining flexibility in promotional activity.

Final Takeaway

Smith & Wesson Brands, Inc. reported strong operational discipline in Q2 2026, highlighted by significant inventory reduction, robust new product momentum, and clean channel inventory positioning. Management projects third quarter sales growth of 8% to 10% over the prior year, supported by expectations of higher gross margins and continued healthy cash generation. The company remains focused on innovation, operational efficiency, and capital discipline to drive future growth, while monitoring macroeconomic and tariff-related risks as it enters its seasonally strongest period.

data.title: Humana, Mark Cuban potentially teaming up for pharmacy partnership
data.description:
• Humana (HUM) and billionaire businessman Mark Cuban, one of the founders of discount online pharmacy Cost Plus Drugs, are working on a potential partnership.
  • Speaking at the Forbes Healthcare Summit in New York Thursday, Cuban and Humana CEO Jim Rechtin said that the arrangement would simplify the drug supply chain.
  • Cuban said that the discussions have dealt with "putting together direct-to-employer programs," Bloomberg reported.
  • Cost Plus Drugs aims to undermine pharmacy benefit managers, the entities that traditionally run prescription drug programs for insurers, by eliminating rebates and spread pricing, providing drugs at a savings to patients directly.
  • President Trump has been a vocal opponent of PBMs, who are colloquially known as "drug middlemen." The PBM industry, in response to criticism, has reportedly been working on reforms to assuage regulators.
